channel.title: DailyNewsRun WebRSS Feed
channel.description: DailyNewsRun WebRSS feed with source links and text previews.

item.title: Utility companies to spend $1.4 trillion over the next 5 years amid data center boom - CBS News
item.description: Updated on: April 14, 2026 / 3:37 PM EDT / CBS News U.S. utility companies are planning to invest $1.4 trillion over the next five years to update the nation's ailing power grid as the data center boom intensifies the need for electricity. That's according to a new report released Tuesday by the nonpartisan nonprofit consumer education organization PowerLines, which analyzed capital spending plans from 51 investor-owned utilities. A majority of those companies, which serve 250 million U.S. customers, cited data centers as a top driver of capital expenditures in their earnings reports.

item.title: Data centers undertake great Midwest migration in search of power - Fierce Network
item.description: Data centers undertake great Midwest migration in search of power Fierce Network

item.title: Utility investment plans jump 21%, further threatening affordability: PowerLines
item.description: “It’s like a gold rush,” said Southern Alliance for Clean Energy Executive Director Stephen Smith. Utilities are proposing investments to meet load growth that at times is “pure speculation,” he said. Share Copy link Email LinkedIn X/Twitter Facebook Print Add us on Google

item.title: Apollo's Jim Zelter on AI Disruption and the Future of Private Credit
item.description: Apollo Global Management President Jim Zelter talks about the unprecedented surge in capital expenditures, the future of private credit, and where he’s seeing investment opportunities around the world. This episode was recorded on March 11, 2026. The opinions and views expressed herein are as of the date of publication, subject to change without notice, and may not necessarily reflect the institutional views of Goldman Sachs or its affiliates. The material provided is intended for informational purposes only, and does not constitute investment advice, a recommendation from any Goldman Sachs entity to take any particular action, or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any securities or financial products. This material may contain forward-looking statements. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Neither Goldman Sachs nor any of its affiliates make any representa...

item.title: Texas power demand to more than quadruple by 2032 as data center boom rolls on, ERCOT says - San Antonio Express-News
item.description: ERCOT is forecasting a record-breaking summer of energy demand, with the grid operator attributing the rapid growth in demand to the influx of data centers coming to Texas. Electricity usage in Texas is expected to smash records this summer as the state grid operator braces for peak demand to more than quadruple by 2032, boosted by data centers and other big users flocking to the state, population growth and increasingly severe weather. This summer’s expected 15% increase over the demand record set in August 2023 was reported by the Electric Reliability Council of Texas in its long-term load forecast issued Thursday. The grid operator is projecting this summer’s peak to reach 98,000 megawatts, up from the 85,508 megawatts Texans used to stay cool Aug. 10, 2023. ERCOT said it has ample supply to meet this summer’s demand.

item.title: Data Centers, Power Markets, and the Economy: How the AI Buildout is Reshaping Energy Costs and the Grid - JD Supra
item.description: Labor & Employment Finance & Banking Intellectual Property

item.title: Energy demand in ComEd territory could double by 2040, in part driven by data centers - Capitol News Illinois
item.description: Article Summary The House Executive Committee met Wednesday to hear from utility companies, consumer advocates and the Illinois Power Agency about how data centers are affecting energy supply, demand and prices. The subject matter hearing was the second of three planned by the committee to gather stakeholders in discussions about data centers and their impacts. The first focused on community impacts, and the third will focus on water. Representatives talked about how data centers have increased demand and what tools are in place to control costs. They also considered the impact on the state’s clean energy goals and the future of renewable energy. This summary was written by the reporters and editors who worked on this story. Editor’s note: This story has been updated to clarify Sarah Moskowitz’s role as the executive director of the Citizens Utility Board. A House committee got an ove...

item.title: Energy demand in ComEd territory could double by 2040, in part driven by data centers - Daily Herald
item.description: Energy demand in ComEd territory could double by 2040, in part driven by data centers Daily Herald

item.title: Sweating It Out: Data Centers Plus Summer Heat Raises Power Bills, Blackout Risk - The Daily Upside
item.description: The US electrical grid isn’t ready for the double whammy of peak air conditioner usage and peak data center power needs. What happens now? Sign up for smart news, insights, and analysis on the biggest financial stories of the day. Brian Boyle

item.title: Global 2025 Power Demand Rose as EV, Data Centers Grew, IEA Says - Bloomberg.com
item.description: Global 2025 Power Demand Rose as EV, Data Centers Grew, IEA Says Bloomberg.com

item.title: Data center power demand will double by 2030: IEA - W.Media
item.description: Data center power demand will double by 2030: IEA Electricity demand from data centers surged 17 percent in 2025, more than five times faster than overall global demand growth of 3 percent, as tech companies spent over US$ 400 billion on AI infrastructure, a figure that could increase by another 75 percent in 2026, according to a new report by the International Energy Agency (IEA). The agency projects that electricity demand from data centers will double by 2030, with demand linked specifically to AI expected to triple over the same period. The Key Questions on Energy and AI report, details the mounting concerns that rapid global expansion of artificial intelligence (AI) is placing significant strain on energy generation and supply systems. While efficiency gains are reducing the energy required per AI task, total consumption continues to rise. This is driven by the scale of deploymen...

item.title: Average US electricity prices rose 9% year over year in February: EIA
item.description: Revenue per kilowatt hour for electricity — a proxy for retail rates — rose by 26.3% in Virginia, 21.9% in Ohio and 19.5% in Pennsylvania, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration. Share Copy link Email LinkedIn X/Twitter Facebook Print Add us on Google

item.title: Why AI data centers are overwhelming the US power grid - Interesting Engineering
item.description: AI shattered the forecasting models utilities have relied on for decades — and the gas plants going up today will outlast every net-zero deadline on the books. Global data center power demand is projected to hit 84 GW by 2027 —a 50 percent jump from 2023 levels—with AI workloads accounting for 27 percent of that total, according to Goldman Sachs Research. The grid cannot keep up with AI. For decades, electricity demand grew slowly and predictably, giving utilities comfortable margins to plan capacity years in advance. That model broke almost overnight. Between 2023 and 2024 alone, utilities’ five-year summer peak demand forecasts jumped from 38 GW to 128 GW , a more than threefold increase in a single planning cycle.
